ABC Radio Sydney

Topic:Animals

Masonry bricks have "frog-sized" holes in them, as Anthony Waddle puts it.(ABC Radio Sydney: Declan Bowring)

At the bottom of a closed quarry next to Sydney Olympic Park is the frontier of the fight to save an Australian animal carrying the national sporting colours.

The green and golden bell frog is endangered in New South Wales and one of the largest remaining populations in the state lives at the old brickpit.

Green and golden bell frogs have brighter colours in warmer months.(Supplied: Anthony Waddle)

When ABC Radio Sydney visited the brickpit, which is closed to the public, we had to navigate boggy 4WD dirt trails and have our boots cleaned down with a bleach solution.

Protection from the neighbouring modern world makes for ideal conditions to conserve the frog, says Macquarie University biologist Anthony Waddle.

"This is great habitat for the frogs to breed in, for tadpoles to grow up in," Dr Waddle said.

Scattered around the quarry are the weapons used in the battle to save the frogs from the killer chytrid fungus, which has been linked to the extinction of 90 frog species around the world.

Dozens of small greenhouses dot the landscape, each containing a tower of black-painted masonry bricks.

It's a simple solution built from basic things bought from a hardware warehouse.

The greenhouses are known as "frog saunas" because of the way they heat up in sunlight.

A green and golden bell frog found by Dr Waddle at the Sydney Olympic Park brickpit.(ABC Radio Sydney: Declan Bowring)

Chytrid is at its most deadly in the winter. Combating the fungus requires a solution that is inviting for the frogs and keeps them warm.

As it happens, standard masonry bricks have "frog-sized" holes in them, as Dr Waddle describes them, and the greenhouses warm them up when in contact with direct sunlight.

The materials that make up "frog saunas" can be found at hardware stores.(ABC Radio Sydney: Declan Bowring)

In Dr Waddle's research published in the journalNature, frogs not only were attracted to the saunas but became more resilient to chytrid and were resistant to subsequent infections.

"We found that if you can simply keep the frogs a bit warm in winter you can prevent them from dying.

"Having high humidity and heat is really key to attracting the frogs and also getting rid of their chytrid infections."

Dozens of small greenhouses are scattered around the old brickpit.(ABC Radio Sydney: Declan Bowring)

While ABC Radio Sydney was visiting the site with Dr Waddle, he found the first green and yellow bell frog that had naturally made a home inside one of his frog saunas.

It's a fitting new chapter in the history of bricks, many of which were made in the same pit the research is taking place in.

The brickpit was saved from development before the Olympics.(ABC Radio Sydney: Declan Bowring)

It's also a fitting new chapter for the area, which was spared from development before the 2000 Sydney Olympics when the endangered frogs were discovered at the site.

Dr Waddle's researchmade a big splash in June, attracting coverage in international news outlets including the New York Times and BBC, with the name "frog saunas" bringing viral fame.

He is pleased by how widely his work has spread, connecting him with fans including a pottery club on the NSW South Coast looking to make frog saunas in their area.

Dr Waddle hopes the viral moment will help propel his simple solution around the country.(ABC Radio Sydney: Declan Bowring)

He is encouraging the public to get involved and has published anonline guide of how to build the saunas.

But he admits he'd like his viral moment to result in some paid work to keep fighting chytrid fungus.

"I want to be able to continue doing this work," Dr Waddle said.

"Chytrid is worldwide, it's caused so much devastation."

title: Frog saunas in old Sydney brickpit lead fight against killer fungus - ABC News
description: At least 90 species of frogs have been made extinct by chytrid fungus worldwide. But a program to protect other frogs with  "saunas" made of bricks is proving promising.
keywords: olympic park green houses, sydney olympic park brickpit, frog saunas olympic park, frog homes, chytrid fungus, macquarie university frog saunas, frog saunas australia, frogs, endangered frogs, green and golden bell frog, bricks, goodnews
